title: unordered_map
description: unordered_map's DSA problem-solving patterns beyond Two Sum: first non-repeating character, and grouping items by a computed key.
objectives: Solve the first-non-repeating-character problem with two passes over a frequency map
Group elements by a computed key using unordered_map<Key, vector<Value>>
Recognize when a problem's shape signals 'use a hash map'
Distinguish this DSA usage from the STL container mechanics already covered

sections.text: 1. Recap: Container Mechanics vs Problem Patterns
The earlier unordered_map topic covered its interface — [], count(), the []-creates-entry gotcha. Hashing then applied it to Two Sum. Here we look at two more classic shapes unordered_map solves.

sections.title: Why two passes
points: Pass 1 builds the complete frequency count for every character — recall unordered_map's freq[c]++ pattern
Pass 2 walks the string again, in ORIGINAL order, checking each character's final count
'swiss': s appears 3 times, w appears 1, i appears 1 — but w comes first in the string, so it's the answer
Overall: O(n) time (two linear passes), O(1) extra space in practice (bounded by the character set size, not the string length)

sections.text: 3. Pattern: Grouping by a Computed Key

This groups anagrams: words that are rearrangements of each other produce the same sorted key. groups["aet"] collects {"eat","tea","ate"}, groups["ant"] collects {"tan","nat"}, groups["abt"] collects {"bat"} — 3 distinct groups. Notice unordered_map<string, vector<string>> — the value type doesn't have to be a simple int; it can be an entire container, exactly matching pair and container-of-containers ideas from earlier.
4. Recognizing the Signal: 'Use a Hash Map'

sections.title: Common signals a hash map applies
points: "Have I seen this before?" — Two Sum, duplicate detection
"How many times does X occur?" — first non-repeating character, frequency-based problems
"Group things that share some computed property" — anagram grouping, categorizing by a derived key
Any time an O(n²) nested-loop comparison could instead become an O(1) lookup

sections.title: Sorting mail into labeled bins by zip code
sections.text: A mail sorting facility doesn't compare every letter to every other letter — it computes each letter's zip code (the key) and drops it straight into the matching bin. Grouping anagrams works the same way: compute each word's 'sorted letters' key, and every word with that same key lands in the same bin automatically.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to find the first non-repeating character in a single pass without first knowing every character's final count — the second pass, in original order, is what makes 'first' correct.
Forgetting groups[key].push_back(word); relies on []'s auto-creation behavior — a missing key gets a fresh empty vector<string> automatically before push_back() runs.
Choosing an unstable or inconsistent grouping key — sorting the characters works because any anagram of a word produces the identical sorted string.
Reaching for nested loops when a 'count things' or 'group things' problem signals a hash map instead.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How would you find the first non-repeating character in a string?
questions.answer: Build a frequency count of every character in one pass, then walk the string again in original order and return the first character whose count is exactly 1.

questions.question: How can unordered_map be used to group anagrams together?
questions.answer: Compute a canonical key for each word, such as its characters sorted alphabetically, then use unordered_map<string, vector<string>> to collect all words sharing that key into the same group.

questions.question: What kinds of problems typically signal that a hash map is the right tool?
questions.answer: Problems asking 'have I seen this before,' 'how many times does this occur,' or 'group items by a shared property' — situations where a hash map turns an O(n²) comparison into O(1) lookups.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Beyond Two Sum, unordered_map solves two other classic shapes: finding the first non-repeating character, using a frequency count built in one pass and checked against the original string order in a second pass, and grouping anagrams, using each word's sorted-character key mapped to unordered_map<string, vector<string>> so every word sharing that key collects into the same group. Both rely on the same underlying signal: whenever a problem asks 'how many times' or 'group by a shared property,' a hash map usually turns an O(n²) brute-force comparison into an O(n) pass.

sections.title: Trick to Remember unordered_map (DSA)
sections.text: First non-repeat = count everything, then re-walk in order. Group anagrams = compute a shared key (sorted letters), drop into the matching bin like sorting mail by zip code.

sections.title: Two Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target.
hints: unordered_map's [] operator and count() are exactly the tools needed to check 'have I seen target - nums[i] before, and at what index?'
Insert AFTER checking, not before — otherwise a number could incorrectly pair with itself.
One pass, one map, O(n) time.

approaches.label: Brute Force
Optimal
approaches.explanation: unordered_map<value, index>, checked before each insertion.

sections.title: Valid Anagram
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s.
hints: unordered_map<char, int>[] auto-creates a 0 entry the first time a new character is seen — exactly what's needed for a running tally.
Increment for s, decrement for t, in two separate loops over the map's keys.
Iterate the map at the end checking every value is exactly 0.

approaches.label: Brute Force
Optimal
approaches.explanation: unordered_map tally, incremented for s and decremented for t.

sections.title: Isomorphic Strings
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return true if the characters of s can be replaced to get t, with a consistent one-to-one mapping.
hints: Two unordered_map<char, char> instances track the mapping in both directions — s to t, and t to s.
[]'s auto-creation means checking count() first tells you whether a mapping already exists before comparing it.
A conflict in either direction immediately disqualifies the strings.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every pair of positions for consistency.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two unordered_map instances enforcing the bijection in a single pass.

sections.title: Word Pattern
sections.problemStatement: Given a pattern and a string s, return true if s follows the same pattern — a bijection between pattern characters and words.
hints: unordered_map<char, string> and unordered_map<string, char> handle the two directions of this bijection — same idea as Isomorphic Strings, but keys/values can be whole words.
Tokenize s into words first (stringstream), matching pattern.length() to words.size().
Check and update both maps together at each position.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Tokenize, then check every pair of positions for consistency.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two unordered_map instances (char->string and string->char) enforcing the bijection in one pass.

sections.title: Ransom Note
sections.problemStatement: Given strings ransomNote and magazine, return true if ransomNote can be constructed using the letters in magazine, each letter used at most as many times as it appears in magazine.
hints: This is a 'do I have ENOUGH of each letter' question — a frequency tally of magazine answers it directly.
Tally every letter's count in magazine first, using unordered_map<char, int>.
Then walk ransomNote, decrementing the count for each letter used — if a needed letter's count ever hits 0 (or was never available), it's impossible.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each character in ransomNote, count its occurrences fresh in both strings and compare.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tally magazine's letters once with unordered_map, then decrement while scanning ransomNote, failing fast if any letter runs out.

sections.title: First Unique Character in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, return the index of its first non-repeating character, or -1 if none exists.

examples.explanation: 'v' is the first character appearing exactly once.

hints: This is exactly this lesson's Section 2 pattern — the only difference is returning an INDEX instead of the character itself.
Pass 1: tally every character's total count with unordered_map<char, int>.
Pass 2: walk the string again in original order, returning the index of the first character whose count is exactly 1.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each character, count its occurrences across the whole string from scratch.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two passes with a frequency map: tally everything first, then find the first index with count 1.

sections.title: Group Anagrams
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's Section 3 grouping-by-computed-key example.
unordered_map<string, vector<string>> — the sorted word is the key, the list of matching original words is the value.
groups[key].push_back(word) relies on []'s auto-creation to start a fresh vector the first time a new key appears.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Compare each string against the first member of every existing group.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: unordered_map<string, vector<string>> keyed by sorted letters, filled in one pass.

sections.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the total number of contiguous subarrays whose sum equals k.
hints: Recall Prefix Sum: a subarray's sum from l to r equals prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. A subarray sums to k exactly when prefix[r] - prefix[l-1] == k.
Rearranged, that's prefix[l-1] == prefix[r] - k — so at each index r, the question becomes 'how many earlier prefix sums equal prefix[r] - k?'
unordered_map<prefixSum, count> tracks how many times each prefix sum has occurred so far — the exact combination of Prefix Sum's technique with this lesson's frequency-counting pattern.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check every subarray's sum directly with nested loops.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A running prefix sum combined with a hash map counting how many times each prefix sum value has occurred — at each index, add the count of (currentSum - k) seen so far.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the prefix sum count map
